topic: Why people obey authority even when they should not — Milgram

script.title: Why You'd Obey Even When You Shouldn't

scenes.narration: In 1961, Stanley Milgram ran an experiment that disturbed the world. He wasn't studying cruelty. He was studying something far more common — obedience.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir: a heavy iron door with a padlock, a single bare bulb swinging overhead casting hard shadows, institutional corridor

scenes.narration: Participants were told to administer electric shocks to a stranger in the next room. The shocks weren't real. But the screams were convincingly recorded.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir: a pair of hands hovering over a control panel of switches and dials, fingers trembling, deep shadows across the frame

scenes.narration: When the voltage reached dangerous levels, most participants hesitated. They sweated. They protested. Then they turned the dial anyway — because an authority figure told them to.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir: a dial turned to maximum, cracked and worn, reflected in a fractured mirror, shadows pooling beneath it

scenes.narration: 65% delivered what they believed were lethal shocks to a complete stranger. Not monsters. Ordinary people — teachers, clerks, fathers — just following instructions.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir: an empty chair with leather restraint straps, single spotlight, the rest of the room swallowed by darkness

scenes.narration: Milgram identified the key: when you feel like a cog in a larger system, your sense of personal responsibility nearly disappears. You stop asking 'Is this right?' and start asking 'Is this my job?'
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir: interlocking gears, one smaller gear being crushed between two massive ones, harsh rim lighting

scenes.narration: This is called the agentic state — the psychological shift where you see yourself as an instrument of authority rather than an autonomous person.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir: a marionette hanging from strings attached to an unseen hand above, the puppet's shadow elongated and distorted on the floor

scenes.narration: The Milgram effect is alive today. It explains why soldiers follow illegal orders. Why workers falsify records. Why bystanders do nothing when a boss is abusive.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir: a long line of identical closed doors receding into darkness, each with a small peephole, light seeping from under only one

scenes.narration: The most terrifying finding wasn't that evil people do evil things. It's that good people do terrible things the moment someone with a clipboard tells them to.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir: a clipboard and pen resting on a desk, a single key beside it, hard shadows splitting the image diagonally, window bars reflected on the wall
